title: ROK Military Strengthens Surveillance Posture as DPRK Missile Testing Tempo Accelerates
summary: South Korea's Joint Chiefs of Staff maintained heightened surveillance and readiness posture following North Korea's sustained missile testing campaign in April 2026, closely coordinating intelligence sharing with US and Japanese forces.
contents:
## ROK Military Readiness Update

### Enhanced Surveillance
South Korea's Joint Chiefs of Staff maintains an elevated surveillance posture and readiness to respond to provocations from North Korea following the country's sustained missile testing campaign throughout April 2026. Intelligence sharing coordination with US and Japanese military counterparts continues at an intensive pace.

### DPRK Testing Timeline (April 2026)
- April 7-8: KN-23 and unidentified missile tests, including suspected cluster munition warhead
- April 12: Cruise missile and anti-ship missile tests from destroyer Choe Hyon
- April 19: Five Hwasong-11D launches with cluster and fragmentation warheads from Sinpo area
- Multiple SRBM launches detected flying 140-900 kilometers toward eastern waters

### ROK Defense Response Measures
- National Security Council convened emergency meetings following each major launch event
- ROK military assessed potential submarine-launched ballistic missile dimensions of Sinpo-area tests
- Coordination with US Forces Korea (USFK) on combined defense posture adjustments
- South Korean intelligence services elevated assessment of Kim Jong Un's daughter as potential successor

### Equipment and Readiness
The ROK armed forces continue modernization programs including accelerated F-35 deployments and expanded Patriot missile defense coverage. The KORDEF 2026 Korea Defense Symposium held at Camp Humphreys from April 21-22 showcased combined defense capabilities.

### Diplomatic Track
President Lee Jae Myung's engagement with China's President Xi Jinping sought a "constructive role" from Beijing in promoting Korean Peninsula peace, though the effectiveness of Chinese influence on Pyongyang remains uncertain.
